surface.name: Getting started with Pipelines, stage 1: Buildkite Starter example
surface.selection_basis: The official Getting started with Pipelines tutorial explicitly divides onboarding into three stages. Stage 1 creates and runs a Buildkite-provided example to teach the New Pipeline page and show how a build runs. Its terminal action is to inspect the completed build's Annotations tab. That is the earliest complete, observable tutorial success before the guide asks the developer to create a repository-backed pipeline and Test Engine suite.

alternatives_considered.name: Create your own pipeline
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is stage 2 of the same tutorial and requires the developer's own repository, task, pipeline.yml, source-control connection, and new build.

alternatives_considered.name: Add a Test Engine suite
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is stage 3 after an operating pipeline and adds framework-specific collector and API-token setup.

alternatives_considered.name: Self-hosted agent onboarding
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The personal signup path creates hosted queues and agents, so the Starter example can run before self-hosted installation.

alternatives_considered.name: Buildkite APIs and CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: API and CLI workflows require separate credentials and developer intent and are not the ordered Pipelines getting-started path.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create a free personal Buildkite account, run the vendor-provided Starter pipeline, and inspect its completed annotation.
assumed_prior_state: The developer does not have a Buildkite account or organization.
No source-code repository, Buildkite agent, pipeline definition, test suite, or paid plan is assumed.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Create a new pipeline from one of the Buildkite-provided examples, to learn the New Pipeline page and see how a build runs.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Create a personal Buildkite account and organization, select the Buildkite Examples Starter pipeline, run its first build on the default hosted environment, wait for completion, and inspect the annotation.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The Starter build completes and its Annotations tab displays the content of the example repository's .buildkite/annotation.md file.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The guide names stage 1 as creating a new pipeline from a Buildkite-provided example, then says to check the completed build's Annotations tab.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the guide's first self-contained success with an observable build result. The next headings separately start the developer's own pipeline and Test Engine suite.

prerequisites.requirement: A GitHub identity that can authorize Buildkite, or an email address, full name, and password for email signup.
Browser and network access to Buildkite, and email inbox access when choosing email signup.
For GitHub signup, permission to authorize Buildkite and, if prompted, install the Buildkite GitHub App for all or selected repositories.

primary_path.action: Open Getting started with Pipelines and follow the free personal-account path.
details: The guide is for developers new to Buildkite Pipelines.
It directs the developer to Start building for free.
primary_path.input: Intent to run a first Buildkite pipeline.
primary_path.success_signal: GitHub and email signup options are available.

details: The email route is preserved as a complete branch.
primary_path.success_signal: Buildkite requests access to the GitHub account.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: GitHub account or authorization problems stop this branch.

primary_path.action: Authorize Buildkite to access the GitHub account.
details: The permission handoff occurs in GitHub.
primary_path.success_signal: Authorization returns the developer toward Buildkite onboarding.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The developer can deny authorization or lack permission.

primary_path.action: If GitHub shows Install Buildkite, choose repository access and complete installation.
details: Select All repositories, which the guide recommends, or Only select repositories to limit scope.
This step appears only if GitHub prompts for the installation.
primary_path.output: Buildkite GitHub App installed at the chosen scope.
primary_path.success_signal: The installation completes and the New Pipeline flow becomes available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Organization policy or missing GitHub administration can require approval or block installation.

primary_path.action: Create the default personal Buildkite environment and open New Pipeline.
details: The guide says either signup path reaches New Pipeline.
For personal accounts, signup defaults include a Default cluster and five hosted queues: linux-small, linux-medium, linux-large, macos-medium, and macos-large.
linux-small is the default hosted queue.
primary_path.output: Buildkite organization, cluster, queues, and New Pipeline page.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Provisioning duration or retry behavior is not documented.

primary_path.action: Switch the New Pipeline Git scope to Buildkite Examples if needed.
details: GitHub signup normally preselects the account and most recently updated repository.
Select Or try an example to use examples.
If the GitHub account has no repositories, Starter pipeline is automatically selected.
primary_path.success_signal: Buildkite Examples appears as the selected scope.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A different scope changes the repository, permissions, and pipeline.

primary_path.action: Select Starter pipeline in the Repository field.
details: Email signup presents Starter pipeline by default.
The example contains a pipeline definition retrieved from the example repository.
primary_path.output: Starter pipeline repository and generated form values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing another example changes steps and annotation.

primary_path.action: Review the New Pipeline fields and keep tutorial defaults.
details: Required concepts include Git scope, Repository, checkout method where applicable, Pipeline name, Default Branch, optional Description, Teams if configured, Cluster, and YAML Steps editor.
For the tutorial, leave Default Branch, Teams when shown, and Cluster unchanged.
primary_path.success_signal: The generated pipeline name, branch, cluster, and YAML are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A duplicate pipeline name produces an error.

primary_path.action: Inspect the Starter YAML's three steps and dependencies.
details: Note build, test, and deploy.
Note their dependency order.
Note the annotation-related command in deploy.
primary_path.output: Understood build graph and expected annotation.
primary_path.success_signal: All three steps and their ordering are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Editing or replacing the template changes the documented example.

details: This creates the Starter pipeline and immediately runs its first build.
A Buildkite pipeline does not commit to the selected repository unless explicitly instructed.
primary_path.output: Created pipeline and running first build.
primary_path.success_signal: The build page opens and jobs begin.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Pipeline-name, configuration, service, or hosted-agent errors can prevent the run.

primary_path.action: Dispatch and run the Starter build jobs on available agents.
details: Pipeline steps become jobs and run on agents.
The Starter pipeline follows build, test, and deploy dependencies.
primary_path.input: Created build and default hosted queue.
primary_path.success_signal: The build reaches a completed state.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Queue capacity, agent, checkout, command, or platform errors can fail or delay the build.

primary_path.action: Open the completed build's Annotations tab.
details: The guide directs this check after the build completes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A failed or unfinished deploy step may prevent the expected annotation.

details: The tab should display the content of the example repository's .buildkite/annotation.md file.
primary_path.success_signal: The expected annotation content is rendered.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing content means the documented example result has not been reached.

branches.path: Select Continue with Email, enter Full name, Email address, and Password, select Sign up, follow the Verify your email page instructions, open the verification email, select its verification link, enter a name on Create organization, and continue to New Pipeline. The page presents the Buildkite Examples Starter pipeline by default.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces GitHub authorization and App installation with form entry, inbox verification, and explicit organization naming.

branches.condition: The developer limits GitHub App access.
branches.path: Choose Only select repositories and select the allowed repositories instead of All repositories. For the vendor example, then switch to Or try an example and Buildkite Examples.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Narrows future repository access but does not prevent the public Starter example.

branches.condition: The GitHub account has no repositories.
branches.path: Use the automatically selected Buildkite Examples Starter pipeline without choosing Or try an example.

branches.condition: The developer selects another Buildkite Example.
branches.path: Choose another example repository and inspect its generated YAML, create and run it, then verify its documented output instead of the Starter annotation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the pipeline jobs and completion content, so it is not the normalized Starter milestone.

friction_gates.description: Signup branches between GitHub authorization and email/password plus email verification.
GitHub signup requires authorizing Buildkite to access the account.
Installing the GitHub App in an organization can require repository-owner or administrator permission.
GitHub signup preselects a repository, so the developer must switch to Buildkite Examples to follow the normalized path.
Pipeline name must not match an existing pipeline name; generated repository, branch, cluster, and YAML choices affect execution.
The developer must wait for hosted agents to run the dependency-ordered build before checking annotations.

sources.title: Getting started with Pipelines
evidence_supported: Full GitHub and email signup routes

sources.title: Defining your pipeline steps
evidence_supported: Manual connection difference for other providers

uncertainties.question: Does a GitHub organization require administrator approval for the Buildkite App installation?
uncertainties.impact: This can add another actor and wait before New Pipeline.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide documents the Install Buildkite prompt and repository-scope choice but cannot know each GitHub organization's App policy.

uncertainties.question: What exact validation, password, and verification-error states occur during current email signup?
uncertainties.impact: Those screens can add retries or prevent account activation.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official guide lists the fields and verification link but does not enumerate error and recovery states.

uncertainties.question: How long does the hosted Starter build take or queue?
uncertainties.impact: The first-success time includes an unbounded execution wait.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: No duration or service-level claim is provided for this example.

excluded_after_success: Creating .buildkite/pipeline.yml in the developer's own repository and committing it.
Connecting a private GitLab, Bitbucket, or other remote repository and configuring webhooks or SSH keys.
Creating the developer's own pipeline and manually starting My first build.
Creating a Test Engine suite, configuring a framework collector and API token, and automating test results.
Self-hosted agent installation and lifecycle management.
Inviting a team, secrets, complex pipelines, plugins, APIs, CLI, and coding-agent integrations.
